Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth. I mostly want to know we’re not missing something obvious, like if one of us can’t work or costs jump.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep it plain-English and we can go deeper where you want. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income is coming into the household in a year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 218600 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what’s the main source of that—wages, business income, rental income, a mix?
Client 1: It’s rental income, paid monthly. It’s basically the whole 218600 annually.

Client 2: I’m employed, yes. But honestly Rober tracks the totals better than I do.
Advisor: No problem. Switching to the outflow side—what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the usual.
Client 2: We’re around 7200 a month—like, total monthly expenses are 7200.
Advisor: Okay. And within that, do you have any monthly debt payments—credit cards, car loans, student loans—anything like that?
Client 2: I mean we pay cards, but that’s just… you know, we pay them off.

Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth. I mostly want to know we’re not missing something obvious, like if one of us can’t work or costs jump.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep it plain-English and we can go deeper where you want. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income is coming into the household in a year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 218600 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what’s the main source of that—wages, business income, rental income, a mix?
Client 1: It’s rental income, paid monthly. It’s basically the whole 218600 annually.

Client 1: Yes—monthly debt payments total is 0. No loans outstanding.
Advisor: Perfect. One second—just noting that down. Now, on the balance sheet side: roughly how much do you have in investable assets—things like brokerage accounts, retirement accounts, cash you’d invest, private investments—excluding the home you live in?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 611200.
Advisor: Thanks. When you say investable, is that spread across a few buckets—like taxable brokerage, retirement, and some cash?
Client 1: Yeah. There’s a joint taxable brokerage account, a retirement account in my name, some cash at a bank, and a private markets piece.

Client 2: And the house, but that’s not really investable, right?
Advisor: Exactly—home equity is important, but I’ll keep it separate from investable assets. Since you brought it up, do you own your primary residence outright?
Client 1: Yes, no mortgage. The primary residence value is 432300.
Advisor: Great—so no mortgage liability. For the investable accounts, are most of those jointly held, or mostly in your name, Rober?
Client 1: The taxable brokerage is joint, the cash account is joint, and the retirement and private markets are in my name.

Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth. I mostly want to know we’re not missing something obvious, like if one of us can’t work or costs jump.

Client 2: I always mix this up—like, is that the percent that comes out of every paycheck?
Advisor: Good question. It’s more the top bracket your last dollars fall into, not a flat rate on everything. What bracket do you typically hit, Rober?
Client 1: 37%.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. While we’re on the “big picture,” I want to make sure I’ve got each category covered: income, assets, liabilities, and protection. We already hit the house at 432300 and no debts—anything new on liabilities since we last spoke? Any loans at all?
Client 2: No—still zero loans outstanding.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Elizabeth—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Rober. We’re trying to get a clean plan—retirement timing, and just making sure the numbers actually hang together.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth. I mostly want to know we’re not missing something obvious, like if one of us can’t work or costs jump.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll keep it plain-English and we can go deeper where you want. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—about how much gross income is coming into the household in a year right now?
Client 1: Household gross is 218600 a year.

Client 1: Household gross is 218600 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what’s the main source of that—wages, business income, rental income, a mix?
Client 1: It’s rental income, paid monthly. It’s basically the whole 218600 annually.
Advisor: Okay, monthly rental income driving the annual total—makes sense. Elizabeth, are you working currently as well, or is it mostly the rentals at this point?
Client 2: I’m employed, yes. But honestly Rober tracks the totals better than I do.

Client 2: No, that’s everything.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. That gives me the numbers. Now I want to make sure I’ve got the household basics right, just a couple quick ones.
Advisor: Rober, Elizabeth—are you two legally married, or is it a different arrangement I should reflect?
Client 1: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And country-wise—just confirming for planning and tax assumptions—are we talking US?

Advisor: Got it. And country-wise—just confirming for planning and tax assumptions—are we talking US?
Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one is more about how we invest. When markets get choppy, do you see yourselves as more conservative, moderate, or growth?
Client 2: I’m more cautious, honestly, but as a household we’ve been growth.
Advisor: Okay, that makes sense—one of you wants the buffer, but the overall posture is growth. I’ll note it that way.

Client 2: I’m more cautious, honestly, but as a household we’ve been growth.
Advisor: Okay, that makes sense—one of you wants the buffer, but the overall posture is growth. I’ll note it that way.
Advisor: And then, just so you know how I work—are you looking for this relationship to be through an RIA, a registered investment advisor, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great. Thank you. Let me just sanity-check I didn’t miss anything from the categories we hit a minute ago—income, assets, liabilities, protection.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Elizabeth—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Rober. We’re trying to get a clean plan—retirement timing, and just making sure the numbers actually hang together.

Advisor: Okay. And just to touch income briefly—your household gross income is 218600 per year, and that’s coming in as rental income paid monthly, correct?
Client 1: Correct—rental income, paid monthly, annual amount is 218600.
Advisor: Perfect. And assets—just so I’m not missing any accounts beyond what you listed: joint taxable brokerage at 293950, your retirement account at 169850, joint cash at 54650, your private markets at 92750, and the primary residence at 432300. Anything else hiding out?
Client 2: No, that’s everything.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. That gives me the numbers. Now I want to make sure I’ve got the household basics right, just a couple quick ones.

Client 1: Yes, that’s the one.
Advisor: Okay—thanks, both. I want to zoom in on you individually for a minute, starting with you, Rober. What’s your current work situation—are you working right now or not?
Client 1: I’m unemployed at the moment—so, unemployed.
Advisor: Got it. And when you were working, what general field were you in? Like finance, tech, healthcare—what bucket fits best?
Client 1: Finance. That’s the cleanest way to describe it.

Advisor: Okay—thanks, both. I want to zoom in on you individually for a minute, starting with you, Rober. What’s your current work situation—are you working right now or not?
